nodes.name: Update POP settings
nodes.description: Tool to update POP settings for a Gmail account. Use when you need to configure POP access window or message disposition behavior.

nodes.name: Update Property
nodes.description: Tool to update an existing GA4 Property. Use when you need to modify property settings such as display name, time zone, currency code, or industry category.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Semantic content — user specifies which fields to update by listing field names (e.g., 'display_name,time_zone') or '*' to update all

nodes.name: Update Reply
nodes.description: Tool to update a reply to a comment on a Google Drive file. Use when you need to modify the content of an existing reply.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The new plain text content is semantic information that only a user can provide

nodes.name: Update send-as alias
nodes.description: Tool to update a send-as alias for a Gmail user. Use when you need to modify display name, signature, reply-to address, or SMTP settings for a send-as email address. Gmail sanitizes HTML signatures before saving. Addresses other than the primary can only be updated by service accounts with domain-wide authority.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The email address to update must be specified by the user; it is semantic content identifying which send-as alias to modify

nodes.name: Update Sheet Properties
nodes.description: Tool to update properties of a sheet (worksheet) within a Google Spreadsheet, such as its title, index, visibility, tab color, or grid properties. Use this when you need to modify the metadata or appearance of a specific sheet.

requiresUserInput.rationale: This object contains the semantic properties to update (title, index, visibility, tab color, grid properties), which are naturally specified by the user.

nodes.name: Update Google Meet Space
nodes.description: Updates the settings of an existing Google Meet space. Requires organizer/host privileges and the meetings.space.created OAuth scope. REQUIRED PARAMETER: - name: The space identifier (e.g., 'spaces/jQCFfuBOdN5z'). This is always required to identify which space to update. OPTIONAL PARAMETERS: - config: The new configuration settings to apply (accessType, entryPointAccess, moderation, etc.) - updateMask: Specify which fields to update. If omitted, all provided config fields are updated. Example: To change access type, provide name='spaces/abc123' and config={'accessType': 'OPEN'}

nodes.name: Update Spreadsheet Properties
nodes.description: Tool to update SPREADSHEET-LEVEL properties such as the spreadsheet's title, locale, time zone, or auto-recalculation settings. Use when you need to modify the overall configuration of a Google Spreadsheet. NOTE: To update individual SHEET properties (like renaming a specific sheet/tab), use GOOGLESHEETS_UPDATE_SHEET_PROPERTIES instead.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Semantic specification of which spreadsheet properties to update (title, locale, timeZone, spreadsheetTheme, etc.); user decides what to modify
The actual property values to set (e.g., new title string, locale code, timezone, theme object); user provides the desired configuration

nodes.name: Update Table Row Style
nodes.description: Tool to update the style of a table row in a Google Document. Use when you need to modify the appearance of specific rows within a table, such as setting minimum row height or marking rows as headers.

requiresUserInput.rationale: This is a complex request object containing styling parameters (row height, header flags, etc.) that only a user can specify based on their desired visual formatting.

nodes.name: Update Task (Deprecated)
nodes.description: DEPRECATED: Use GOOGLETASKS_PATCH_TASK instead. Full-update (PUT-style) operation that overwrites unspecified fields with empty/default values, which can cause data loss. Prefer GOOGLETASKS_PATCH_TASK unless a complete field replacement is explicitly required.

nodes.name: Update Task (Full Replacement)
nodes.description: Tool to fully replace an existing Google Task using PUT method. Use when you need to update the entire task resource, not just specific fields. This method requires all required fields (id, title) and replaces the complete task, unlike PATCH which supports partial updates.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The new title text for the task is semantic content only a user can provide

nodes.name: Update Task List
nodes.description: Updates the authenticated user's specified task list.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The new title for the task list is semantic content that only a human can provide

nodes.name: Update Team Drive (Deprecated)
nodes.description: Tool to update a Team Drive's metadata. Deprecated: Use the drives.update endpoint instead. Use when you need to modify Team Drive properties.

nodes.name: Update User Attributes Values
nodes.description: Update user attribute values for a resource. Use this action to set or update custom attributes for a user within an organization or project. When setting a value for an attribute key that also exists in SAML, the Sanity value will take precedence and shadow the SAML value.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The unique identifier of the user must come from a prior tool that lists/gets users or from user lookup
The key-value pairs of custom attributes are semantic content only a human can define; values are strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, or nested objects provided by the user
The resource type (e.g., 'organization' or 'project') is a semantic classification provided by the user

nodes.name: Update Vacation Settings
nodes.description: Tool to update vacation responder settings for a Gmail user. Use when you need to configure out-of-office auto-replies.

nodes.name: Batch update spreadsheet values
nodes.description: Tool to set values in one or more ranges of a spreadsheet. Use when you need to update multiple ranges in a single operation for better performance.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Array of ValueRange objects specifying ranges and values to write; this is semantic configuration only a user can define based on their data update needs
Enum value (RAW, USER_ENTERED, INPUT_VALUE_OPTION_UNSPECIFIED) determining interpretation strategy; user must decide how to treat input data

nodes.name: Upload File
nodes.description: Uploads a file (max 5MB) to Google Drive, placing it in the specified folder or root if no valid folder ID is provided. Always creates a new file (never updates existing); use GOOGLEDRIVE_EDIT_FILE to update with a stable file_id. Uploaded files are private by default; configure sharing via GOOGLEDRIVE_ADD_FILE_SHARING_PREFERENCE.

nodes.name: Upload File from URL to Drive
nodes.description: Tool to fetch a file from a provided URL server-side and upload it into Google Drive. Use when you need to reliably persist externally hosted files into Drive without client-side downloads or temporary storage.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Semantic filename including extension; only the user can specify the desired name for the file in Drive.
The external URL from which to fetch the file. This is a semantic URL string that the user must provide.

nodes.name: Upload Media
nodes.description: Upload a media file to Google Photos. Supports images (up to 200MB) and videos (up to 20GB).

nodes.name: Upload/Update File Content
nodes.description: Tool to update file content in Google Drive by uploading new binary content. Use when you need to replace the contents of an existing file with new file data.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The binary file content to upload; user must provide the actual file data or file object

nodes.name: Upsert Rows (Smart Update/Insert)
nodes.description: Upsert rows - update existing rows by key, append new ones. Automatically handles column mapping and partial updates. Use for: CRM syncs (match Lead ID), transaction imports (match Transaction ID), inventory updates (match SKU), calendar syncs (match Event ID). Features: - Auto-adds missing columns to sheet - Partial column updates (only update Phone + Status, preserve other columns) - Column order doesn't matter (auto-maps by header name) - Prevents duplicates by matching key column Example inputs: - Contact update: keyColumn='Email', headers=['Email','Phone','Status'], data=[['john@ex.com','555-0101','Active']] - Inventory sync: keyColumn='SKU', headers=['SKU','Stock','Price'], data=[['WIDGET-001',50,9.99],['GADGET-002',30,19.99]] - CRM lead update: keyColumn='Lead ID', headers=['Lead ID','Score','Status'], data=[['L-12345',85,'Hot']] - Partial update: keyColumn='Email', headers=['Email','Phone'] (only updates Phone, preserves Name/Address/etc)

requiresUserInput.rationale: 2D array of data values to upsert; semantic content provided by user. Can include IDs from producer tools, but the array structure itself is user-constructed.
Name of the sheet/tab within the spreadsheet. User specifies which sheet to target; may be auto-resolved if default name not found.

nodes.name: Validate Events
nodes.description: Tool to validate Measurement Protocol events before sending them to production. Use when you need to verify event structure and parameters are correct before sending real data.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Array of event objects with names and parameters is semantic content only a user can provide; represents the specific tracking events to validate
Unique client identifier is application-specific configuration that must be provided by the user or obtained from their analytics setup
API secret is a credential from Google Analytics configuration that only the user can supply from their GA account
Measurement ID is a Google Analytics identifier from the user's GA property configuration; specific to their analytics setup

nodes.name: Get spreadsheet values
nodes.description: Returns a range of values from a spreadsheet. Use when you need to read data from specific cells or ranges in a Google Sheet.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The A1 or R1C1 notation range is semantic content specifying which cells to read; only the user knows which data range they need (e.g., 'Sheet1!A1:B3' or 'Sales Data!C5:D20')

nodes.name: Update spreadsheet values
nodes.description: Tool to set values in a range of a Google Spreadsheet. Use when you need to update or overwrite existing cell values in a specific range.

requiresUserInput.rationale: User must specify the A1 notation range to update (e.g., 'Sheet1!A1:C2'). This is semantic cell reference content.
The actual data/content to write into cells. This is semantic content only a user can provide.
User must choose between RAW (literal strings) or USER_ENTERED (parsed as user input). This is a semantic choice about data interpretation.

nodes.name: Watch Drive Changes
nodes.description: Tool to subscribe to changes for a user or shared drive in Google Drive. Use when you need to monitor a Google Drive for modifications and receive notifications at a specified webhook URL. Notifications may be batched rather than per-change; design handlers to be idempotent and fetch all changes since the last known page_token on each notification.

requiresUserInput.rationale: A UUID or unique identifier that the user must provide to identify this specific watch channel
The notification delivery mechanism type (e.g., 'web_hook') is a semantic choice only the user can specify
A publicly reachable HTTPS webhook URL that only the user can supply

nodes.name: Watch File for Changes
nodes.description: Tool to subscribe to push notifications for changes to a specific file. Use when you need to monitor a file for modifications and receive real-time notifications at a webhook URL.

requiresUserInput.rationale: User must provide a unique identifier for this notification channel subscription.
User specifies the delivery mechanism type (e.g., 'web_hook').
User must provide the HTTPS webhook URL where notifications will be delivered.

nodes.name: Abort Repository Migration
nodes.description: Tool to abort a repository migration that is queued or in progress. Use when you need to cancel an ongoing migration operation.

nodes.name: Accept a repository invitation
nodes.description: Accepts a PENDING repository invitation that has been issued to the authenticated user.

nodes.name: Add app access restrictions
nodes.description: Adds GitHub Apps to the list of apps allowed to push to a protected branch. The branch must already have protection rules with restrictions enabled. This endpoint only works for organization repositories, not personal repositories. Apps must be installed on the repository with 'contents' write permissions.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The list of GitHub App slugs (e.g., 'github-actions', 'dependabot') is semantic content that only the user can specify based on which apps they want to grant push access to
The repository name is a reference that identifies a specific repository; while it can be user-provided, in agent workflows it typically comes from prior tool output that produced repo identifiers
The organization name is a reference that identifies the owner entity; while it can be user-provided, in agent workflows it typically comes from prior tool output that produced owner identifiers

nodes.name: Add a repository collaborator
nodes.description: Adds a GitHub user as a repository collaborator, or updates their permission if already a collaborator; `permission` applies to organization-owned repositories (personal ones default to 'push' and ignore this field), and an invitation may be created or permissions updated directly.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name must be provided or sourced from a prior tool that created/listed repositories
The repository owner (account name) must be provided or sourced from a prior tool that identified the account context

nodes.name: Add assignees to an issue
nodes.description: Adds assignees to a GitHub issue. This action only adds users - it does not remove existing assignees. Changes are silently ignored if the authenticated user lacks push access to the repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository owner is typically discovered via a prior tool that lists/searches repositories or retrieves repository details
The repository name is typically discovered via a prior tool that lists/searches repositories

nodes.name: Add email for auth user
nodes.description: Adds one or more email addresses (which will be initially unverified) to the authenticated user's GitHub account; use this to associate new emails, noting an email verified for another account will error, while an existing email for the current user is accepted.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Array of email addresses is semantic content only a human can provide; these are email strings, not references to entities produced by other tools

nodes.name: Add field to user project
nodes.description: Tool to add a custom field to a user-owned GitHub Projects V2 project. Use when you need to add fields like status, priority, or custom data to organize project items.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The field name is semantic content only a user can provide (e.g., 'Status', 'Priority', 'Assignee')
The GitHub username is provided by the user as natural language identifier of the account owner
The field data type (single_select, iteration, text, etc.) is a semantic choice only the user can make based on their project needs

nodes.name: Add item to user project
nodes.description: Tool to add an issue or pull request to a user-owned GitHub project. Use when you need to add existing repository items to a project board.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name; part of the owner+repo+number triplet required when id is not provided. Identifies the repository.
Semantic choice between 'Issue' or 'PullRequest'; only the user can specify what type of item is being added.
Repository owner login; part of the owner+repo+number triplet required when id is not provided. Identifies the account owning the repository.
GitHub user account handle; identifies the user who owns the project. Must be provided explicitly or resolved from context.

nodes.name: Add labels to an issue
nodes.description: Adds labels (provided in the request body) to a repository issue; labels that do not already exist are created.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The username/organization owning the repository must be known; typically comes from a prior tool that listed or created a repo, or user provides it directly as a known GitHub account
Repository name is a reference to a specific repo that must exist or have been identified beforehand
Array of label names is semantic content provided by the user describing which labels should be applied; does not require a producer reference

nodes.name: Add org runner labels
nodes.description: Adds new custom labels to an existing self-hosted runner for an organization; existing labels are not removed, and duplicates are not added.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Organization name is a semantic identifier that only the user can provide; it's case-insensitive but must be known by the user.
The list of custom label names to add is semantic content that only the user can provide based on their labeling strategy.

nodes.name: Add or update team membership for a user
nodes.description: Adds a GitHub user to a team or updates their role (member or maintainer), inviting them to the organization if not already a member; idempotent, returning current details if no change is made.

nodes.name: Add or update team project permissions
nodes.description: Adds a classic project to a team or updates the team's permission on it. This endpoint grants or updates permissions for a team on a specific classic project (not Projects V2). The authenticated user must have admin permissions for the project. Both the team and project must belong to the same organization. Requirements: - The project must be a classic project (not GitHub Projects V2) - The authenticated user must have admin permissions on the project - The team and project must be in the same organization - Requires 'admin:org' scope for the authentication token Returns HTTP 204 No Content on success, 403 if project is not an org project, or 404 if the organization, team, or project is not found.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The organization name is a reference to a GitHub organization that typically comes from prior tool output (e.g., listing repos or teams) or requires lookup validation. While it's a string, it's a specific entity identifier.

nodes.name: Add or update team repository permissions
nodes.description: Sets or updates a team's permission level for a repository within an organization; the team must be a member of the organization.

nodes.name: Add project collaborator
nodes.description: Adds a specified GitHub user as a collaborator to an existing organization project with a given permission level. Note: This endpoint is for organization projects (classic). You must be an organization owner or a project admin to add a collaborator. Classic projects are being deprecated in favor of the new Projects experience.

nodes.name: Add a repository to an app installation
nodes.description: Adds a repository to a GitHub App installation, granting the app access; requires authenticated user to have admin rights for the repository and access to the installation.

nodes.name: Add repo to org secret with selected access
nodes.description: Adds a repository to an existing organization-level GitHub Actions secret that is configured for 'selected' repository access.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Organization name is a semantic identifier provided by the user; it's a string label, not a reference produced by another tool.

nodes.name: Add selected repo to org secret
nodes.description: Grants an existing repository access to an existing organization-level Dependabot secret when the secret's visibility is set to 'selected'; the repository must belong to the organization, and the call succeeds without change if access already exists.

nodes.name: Add runner labels
nodes.description: Adds and appends custom labels to a self-hosted repository runner, which must be registered and active.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is a canonical identifier that must be obtained from a prior tool that lists or retrieves repositories, or provided by the user as a known repository name.
Owner (username or organization) is a canonical identifier that typically comes from repository metadata or must be known/provided by the user.
Custom label names are semantic content provided by the user; they are free-form strings with no external resolution required.

nodes.name: Add selected repository to an organization secret
nodes.description: Adds a repository to an organization secret's access list when the secret's visibility is 'selected'; this operation is idempotent.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Organization name is a semantic identifier that only a user can naturally provide; not an output ID from another tool.
Secret name is a human-readable identifier chosen by the user; not a produced ID from another tool.

nodes.name: Add selected repository to an organization variable
nodes.description: Grants a repository access to an organization-level GitHub Actions variable, if that variable's visibility is set to 'selected_repositories'.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The name of the organization-level GitHub Actions variable is semantic content only a human can specify; it is not an ID that another tool produces.

nodes.name: Add a selected repository to a user secret
nodes.description: Grants a specified repository access to an authenticated user's existing Codespaces secret, enabling Codespaces created for that repository to use the secret.

nodes.name: Add social accounts for the authenticated user
nodes.description: Adds one or more social media links (which must be valid, full URLs for platforms supported by GitHub) to the authenticated user's public GitHub profile.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Social media URLs are semantic content that only a user can naturally provide; they represent the user's choice of which profiles to add to their GitHub profile.

nodes.name: Add status check contexts
nodes.description: Adds status check contexts to a protected branch's required status checks. This action appends new status check contexts to the existing list of required status checks for a protected branch. The branch must already have branch protection enabled with status checks configured. Note: The 'contexts' parameter is deprecated by GitHub in favor of 'checks' array. For new implementations, consider using update_status_check_protection with the 'checks' parameter instead.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The account owner is a GitHub entity reference that typically comes from a prior lookup or list tool, or may be user-provided if they know the exact owner name
The repository name is a reference to a specific repo that must exist and typically originates from a prior search/list tool or user knowledge
The branch name is a semantic identifier that the user provides; it must already exist and have branch protection enabled
The status check contexts are semantic names of CI/CD jobs or checks that the user specifies; these are configuration values, not entity references

nodes.name: Add sub-issue to an issue
nodes.description: Tool to add a sub-issue to a parent GitHub issue using GraphQL. Use when you need to establish a parent-child relationship between issues. Requires the 'sub_issues' GraphQL feature to be enabled.

nodes.name: Add team access restrictions
nodes.description: Adds teams to the list of teams with push access to a protected branch. This action grants additional teams push access to a protected branch. Unlike 'Set team access restrictions' (PUT), this action adds to the existing list rather than replacing it entirely. Prerequisites: - The repository must be owned by an organization (not a personal account) - The branch must have protection rules enabled - The branch protection must have restrictions configured - The teams must exist in the organization and have repository access

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name must be known; typically produced by a tool that lists or retrieves repositories, or provided by user as a known entity reference
Organization name must be known; typically produced by a tool that lists repositories or organizations, or provided by user as a known entity reference
Branch name is semantic content the user specifies; it's a literal branch name, not an ID that requires lookup or production by another tool

nodes.name: Add user access restrictions
nodes.description: Adds users to the list of people allowed to push to a protected branch in an organization repository. Important notes: - This action only works on organization-owned repositories (not personal repos) - The branch must already have protection rules with restrictions enabled - Users must have write access to the repository to be added - The combined total of users, apps, and teams is limited to 100 items

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is a specific identifier that typically comes from a prior tool that listed or created repositories, or from user selection of a known repo.
Organization owner name is a specific identifier for the account that owns the repository, typically resolved via prior lookup or user specification of a known org.
Branch name is a semantic identifier provided by the user; it is a direct string input without requiring prior tool resolution.

nodes.name: Add users to codespaces access for an organization
nodes.description: Adds organization members to the list of users granted Codespaces access billed to the organization. IMPORTANT: This endpoint requires the organization's Codespaces billing access to be set to 'selected_members' mode. If not already configured, use the GITHUB_MANAGE_ACCESS_CONTROL_FOR_ORGANIZATION_CODESPACES action first to set visibility to 'selected_members'.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Organization name is semantic content that only the user can provide. While it could theoretically come from a prior tool, the natural workflow is for the user to specify which organization to configure.

nodes.name: Get GitHub API root
nodes.description: Retrieves a map of all top-level GitHub REST API resource URLs and their templates.

nodes.name: Approve a workflow run for a fork pull request
nodes.description: Approves a workflow run from a forked repository's pull request; call this when such a run requires manual approval due to workflow configuration.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The account owner of the repository; typically a GitHub user or organization that must be identified/resolved from context or prior tool output
The repository name; paired with owner to uniquely identify a GitHub repository, typically obtained from prior tool output or user specification

nodes.name: Assign an organization role to a team
nodes.description: Assigns an existing organization-level role (identified by `role_id`) to a team (identified by `team_slug`) within a GitHub organization (`org`), provided the organization, team, and role already exist.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Organization name is a semantic identifier provided by the user; it is case-insensitive but must be supplied by the user to specify which organization receives the role assignment.

nodes.name: Assign an organization role to a user
nodes.description: Assigns a specific organization role to a user who is a member or an outside collaborator in a GitHub organization, using a valid role ID.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The organization name is a semantic identifier provided by the user; it is not an ID produced by another tool.
The GitHub username is a semantic identifier naturally provided by the user; it is not produced by another tool.

nodes.name: List Docker Migration Conflicting Packages
nodes.description: List Docker packages with migration conflicts for the authenticated user. This endpoint lists all Docker packages owned by the authenticated user that encountered namespace conflicts during the Docker-to-GitHub Container Registry (GHCR) migration. Conflicts occur when a package with the same name exists in both the legacy Docker registry and GHCR. IMPORTANT: The Docker registry for GitHub Packages was deprecated on Feb 24, 2025. This endpoint may return a 400 error with message 'Package migration for docker is no longer supported' as the migration period has ended. In this case, the action returns an informative response instead of failing. Use case: Identifying packages that require manual migration to GHCR. Required scope: read:packages (for OAuth and personal access tokens).

nodes.name: Block a user
nodes.description: Blocks an existing individual GitHub user (not an organization or your own account), preventing them from interacting with your account and repositories.

nodes.name: Block a user from an organization
nodes.description: Blocks a GitHub user from an organization, preventing their contributions, collaboration, and forking of the organization's repositories. Requires admin or 'Blocking users' organization permission (write access).

requiresUserInput.rationale: GitHub username is a specific identifier the user must provide directly; it is a semantic handle rather than an ID produced by another tool.

nodes.name: Cancel a GitHub Pages deployment
nodes.description: Cancels an existing, ongoing or queued GitHub Pages deployment for a repository using its `pages_deployment_id`.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is typically produced by a search/list tool or provided as a reference to a specific repo.
Owner (username or organization) is typically produced by a search/list tool or known from prior context.

nodes.name: Cancel sponsorship
nodes.description: Tool to cancel an active GitHub sponsorship using GraphQL. Use when you need to terminate a sponsorship relationship between a sponsor and a sponsorable entity. Either sponsor ID/login and sponsorable ID/login must be provided.

nodes.name: Cancel a workflow run
nodes.description: Cancels a workflow run in a GitHub repository if it is in a cancellable state (e.g., 'in_progress' or 'queued'). Returns 202 Accepted on success.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is typically obtained from a prior tool that lists or searches repositories, or from a user who references a repo they already know about. In agent workflows, this is often the output of a repository lookup.
The repository owner is typically obtained from a prior tool or user context. In agent workflows, owner and repo are paired identifiers that come from repository discovery/lookup.

nodes.name: Check if a gist is starred
nodes.description: Checks if a gist is starred by the authenticated user. Returns `is_starred: true` if the gist is starred (HTTP 204), or `is_starred: false` if not starred (HTTP 404). Use this to verify star status before starring/unstarring a gist.

nodes.name: Check if pull request merged
nodes.description: Checks if a specified GitHub pull request has been merged, indicated by a 204 HTTP status (merged) or 404 (not merged/found).

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository owner is a GitHub account identifier that typically comes from a prior tool result (e.g., listing repos, getting PR details) or user specification of a known org/user.
The repository name is a reference that typically originates from a prior tool result or user's explicit repo identifier.

nodes.name: Check if a user can be assigned
nodes.description: Verifies if a GitHub user can be assigned to issues in a repository; assignability is confirmed by an HTTP 204 (No Content) response, resulting in an empty 'data' field in the response.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is typically obtained from a prior tool that lists or retrieves repositories, or from a workflow context where repo has been identified.
The account owner (GitHub username or organization name) is typically obtained from a prior tool that identifies the repository owner or from workflow context.

nodes.name: Check if a user can be assigned to an issue
nodes.description: Checks if a specified GitHub user can be assigned to a given issue within a repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is typically produced by a list/get tool or must be known from prior tool output in a workflow
Repository owner is typically produced by a list/get tool or must be known from prior tool output in a workflow

nodes.name: Check if a user follows another user
nodes.description: Checks if a GitHub user `username` follows `target_user`; returns a 204 HTTP status if true, 404 if not or if users are invalid.

nodes.name: Check if a user is blocked by the authenticated user
nodes.description: Checks if the specified GitHub user is blocked by the authenticated user; a 204 No Content response indicates the user is blocked, while a 404 Not Found indicates they are not.

nodes.name: Check if a user is blocked by an organization
nodes.description: Checks if a GitHub user is blocked by an organization. Returns is_blocked=True if the user is blocked (HTTP 204), or is_blocked=False if not blocked (HTTP 404). Requires 'Blocking users' organization permission (read access).

nodes.name: Check if a user is a repository collaborator
nodes.description: Checks if a user is a collaborator on a specified GitHub repository, returning a 204 status if they are, or a 404 status if they are not or if the repository/user does not exist.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is typically obtained from a prior tool that lists or creates repositories, or from a user naming a repo they've already identified.
The repository owner (GitHub username or organization) is typically obtained from a prior tool or user context; it pairs with repo to identify a unique repository.

nodes.name: Check if person is followed by authenticated user
nodes.description: Checks if the authenticated GitHub user follows a target GitHub user. Returns a structured response with is_following=True when following (HTTP 204), or is_following=False when not following (HTTP 404).

nodes.name: Check private vulnerability reporting status
nodes.description: Checks if private vulnerability reporting is enabled for the specified repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The GitHub organization or user that owns the repository. Must be resolved or provided by a prior tool that lists/creates repositories or from user context.
The repository name within the owner's namespace. Typically obtained from a tool that lists or creates repositories, paired with owner.

nodes.name: Check public organization membership
nodes.description: Tool to check if a user is a public member of an organization. Use when you need to verify public organization membership status.

nodes.name: Check team permissions for a project
nodes.description: Checks if a team has 'read', 'write', or 'admin' permissions for an organization's specific classic project, returning the project's details if access is confirmed.

nodes.name: Check team permissions for a repository
nodes.description: Checks a team's permissions for a specific repository within an organization, including permissions inherited from parent teams.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is a canonical entity identifier typically produced by repo search/list tools or provided as part of workflow context
The owner parameter must match org and is a canonical entity identifier for the organization

nodes.name: Check a token
nodes.description: Checks if a GitHub App or OAuth access_token is valid for the specified client_id and retrieves its details, typically to verify its active status and grants. NOTE: This endpoint requires Basic Authentication using the OAuth App's client_id as username and client_secret as password. The access_token parameter must be an OAuth token issued by the specified OAuth App.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The OAuth App's unique client ID is a configuration value provided by the user or stored in environment/secrets; it identifies which OAuth App issued the token being validated
The OAuth access token to validate is a credential/secret that must be provided by the user; it is typically obtained from authentication flows or stored securely

nodes.name: Clear Project V2 Item Field Value
nodes.description: Tool to clear the value of a field for an item in a GitHub Project V2. Use when you need to remove or reset a field value (text, number, date, assignees, labels, single-select, iteration, or milestone fields).

nodes.name: Clear repository cache by key
nodes.description: Deletes GitHub Actions caches from a repository matching a specific `key` and an optional Git `ref`, used to manage storage or clear outdated/corrupted caches; the action succeeds even if no matching caches are found to delete.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The cache key is a semantic identifier that only the user can specify; it represents the exact cache key pattern they want to delete, often dynamically generated in workflows.
The repository name is a required identifier that typically comes from a prior tool that lists or retrieves repositories.
The repository owner is a required identifier that typically comes from a prior tool or user context identifying the GitHub account.

nodes.name: Clear self-hosted runner org labels
nodes.description: Removes all custom labels from a self-hosted runner for an organization; default labels (e.g., 'self-hosted', 'linux', 'x64') will remain.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Organization name is a semantic identifier provided by the user; while it could theoretically be looked up, it is typically known directly by the user.

nodes.name: Commit Multiple Files
nodes.description: Tool to atomically create, update, or delete multiple files in a GitHub repository as a single commit. Uses Git Data APIs to avoid SHA mismatch conflicts that occur with the Contents API when multiple files are modified in parallel. Use when you need to make multi-file changes reliably. BRANCH CREATION: When committing to a new branch (e.g., 'fix/my-fix' or 'feature/new-feature'), you MUST provide 'base_branch' (typically 'main' or 'master') to create the branch from. If the branch already exists, base_branch is not needed. This action handles race conditions automatically: if the branch is updated by another commit between fetching the HEAD and updating the reference (resulting in a 422 'not a fast forward' error), the action will retry by refetching the HEAD and rebasing changes. Use max_retries to control this behavior.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository owner must be a valid GitHub user or organization; typically produced by a prior lookup or repo discovery tool
Repository name must be a valid existing repository; typically produced by a prior repo lookup or discovery tool
Branch name is semantic content specified by the user; can be a new or existing branch name
Commit message is semantic content that only a human can naturally compose to describe the changes

nodes.name: Compare two commits
nodes.description: Compares two commit points (commits, branches, tags, or SHAs) within a repository or across forks, using `BASE...HEAD` or `OWNER:REF...OWNER:REF` format for the `basehead` parameter.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository owner (username or organization) must be provided; typically comes from a prior tool that listed or created a repo, or from user lookup
The repository name must be provided; typically comes from a prior tool that listed or created a repo
The comparison specification (BASE...HEAD) is semantic content that only a human can naturally express, including branch/tag/SHA selection and fork-specific formatting

nodes.name: Configure JIT runner for an org
nodes.description: Generates a JIT configuration for a GitHub organization's new self-hosted runner to run a single job then unregister; requires admin:org scope and the runner_group_id must exist in the organization.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Organization name is semantic content provided by the user; case-insensitive string identifier
Desired runner name is semantic content chosen by the user for display in GitHub UI
Custom labels are semantic content (tags/keywords) provided by user to route jobs; user defines what labels make sense for their workflow

nodes.name: Configure OIDC subject claim template
nodes.description: Sets or updates the OIDC subject claim customization template for an existing GitHub organization by specifying which claims (e.g., 'repo', 'actor') form the OIDC token's subject (`sub`). This action customizes which claim keys are included in the OIDC subject claim for the specified organization. It allows for fine-tuning the content of the subject claim based on organizational needs.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Array of OIDC claim key strings (e.g., 'repo', 'actor') is semantic configuration only the user can specify based on their OIDC requirements

nodes.name: Convert an organization member to outside collaborator
nodes.description: Converts an existing organization member, who is not an owner, to an outside collaborator, restricting their access to explicitly granted repositories.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Organization name is a semantic identifier that only the user can specify; it is not produced by another tool
GitHub username is a semantic identifier that the user must provide; while it could theoretically be looked up, the direct input is the natural usage pattern

nodes.name: Create a blob
nodes.description: Creates a Git blob in a repository, requiring content and encoding ('utf-8' or 'base64'). Requires write access to the repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The GitHub repository owner (username or organization) is a required identifier that must be known or looked up
The GitHub repository name is a required identifier that must be known or looked up
The blob content is semantic text/data that only the user can provide or generate

nodes.name: Create a check run
nodes.description: Creates a new check run for a specific commit in a repository, used by external services to report status, detailed feedback, annotations, and images directly within the GitHub UI. NOTE: This endpoint requires GitHub App authentication - OAuth tokens and personal access tokens cannot create check runs.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The check name is semantic content provided by the user (e.g., 'Code Coverage', 'Linter')
Repository name is a canonical identifier that must be known; typically produced by a repository lookup or list tool
Repository owner is a canonical identifier; typically produced by a repository lookup or list tool

nodes.name: Create a check suite
nodes.description: Creates a new check suite for a specific commit (`head_sha`) in an original repository (not a fork). IMPORTANT: This endpoint requires a GitHub App installation access token - OAuth tokens and classic personal access tokens cannot use this endpoint. GitHub dispatches a `check_suite` webhook event with the `requested` action upon success.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name must be a specific repo that exists; typically obtained from a prior tool that lists or retrieves repositories, or from user selection of a known repo name
Account owner must be a specific GitHub user or organization; typically obtained from a prior tool that identifies the owner, or from user specification of a known account

nodes.name: Create a codespace for the authenticated user
nodes.description: Creates a GitHub Codespace for the authenticated user, requiring a JSON request body with either `repository_id` (integer) or a `pull_request` object (containing `pull_request_number` (integer) and `repository_id` (integer)). This action allows users to set up a development environment quickly by creating a codespace from a specified repository or pull request.

nodes.name: Create a codespace from a pull request
nodes.description: Creates a GitHub Codespace for an open pull request in a Codespaces-enabled repository, with options to customize its configuration.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository owner username must be known; typically from a prior list/get tool or user context. Paired with repo to identify the repository.
Repository name must be known; typically from a prior list/get tool or user context. Paired with owner to identify the repository.

nodes.name: Create a codespace in a repository
nodes.description: Creates a GitHub Codespace for the authenticated user in a specified repository, which must be accessible and use a valid `devcontainer.json` if `devcontainer_path` is specified.

nodes.name: Create a commit
nodes.description: Creates a new commit in a GitHub repository; the `tree` SHA and any `parents` SHAs must already exist in the repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is a reference to an existing GitHub repository; typically produced by list/get tools or looked up by name
Account owner is a reference to a GitHub user/org; typically produced by list/get tools or looked up by name
Commit message is semantic content only a human can naturally provide

nodes.name: Create a commit comment
nodes.description: Creates a comment on a specific commit, or on a specific line if `path` and `position` are provided.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The text content of the comment is semantic information only a human can compose

nodes.name: Create a commit status
nodes.description: Sets a commit's status (e.g., error, failure, pending, success from CI/CD) for a given SHA; max 1000 statuses per SHA/context.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is typically discovered via a prior GitHub list/get operation or provided as a known identifier from workflow context.
Owner (username or organization) is typically discovered via prior GitHub operations or provided as workflow context.
State is a semantic choice (error, failure, pending, success) determined by the agent or user based on CI/CD outcome, not an ID from another tool.

nodes.name: Create a custom organization role
nodes.description: Creates a custom role with defined permissions within a GitHub organization.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Organization name is a semantic identifier provided by the user; while it could theoretically be looked up, it's typically directly specified
The custom role name is semantic content defined by the user

Permissions are a list of fine-grained permission strings that must be provided by the user; the description explicitly recommends using a separate lookup action (List Organization Fine-Grained Permissions) to discover available options, but the actual selection is user-driven

nodes.name: Create a deploy key
nodes.description: Creates a deploy key for a repository; the repository must exist and be accessible, and the provided key must be a valid public SSH key.

requiresUserInput.rationale: A full public SSH key is semantic content only the user can provide; it cannot be derived from other tools
The repository name must be an existing repository, typically resolved or provided by upstream repo listing/creation tools
The repository owner username is typically obtained from upstream GitHub tools that list or create repositories

nodes.name: Create a deployment
nodes.description: Creates a GitHub deployment for an existing repository, targeting a specific ref (branch, tag, or SHA) that must also exist within the repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository owner account must be known; typically provided by user or resolved from a prior operation
The repository name must be known; typically provided by user or resolved from a prior operation

nodes.name: Create a deployment branch policy
nodes.description: Creates a deployment branch or tag policy for an existing environment in a GitHub repository, using a Ruby File.fnmatch pattern (where `*` doesn't match `/`) to specify which branches or tags are deployable.

requiresUserInput.rationale: A semantic pattern string for deployable branches/tags using Ruby File.fnmatch syntax; only a human can specify the desired deployment pattern
Repository name must be known; typically obtained from a prior GitHub tool that lists or creates repositories
Repository owner/account must be known; typically obtained from a prior GitHub tool that identifies the account or repository

nodes.name: Create a deployment status
nodes.description: Creates a status for an existing deployment, updating its operational state, associated URLs, and description.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name must be identified; typically comes from a prior tool that lists or creates repositories, or user provides it by name requiring lookup
Repository owner (account) must be identified; comes from repository context or prior tool output
Semantic state value (e.g., 'pending', 'success', 'failure', 'inactive') only a human can decide based on deployment outcome

nodes.name: Create a discussion
nodes.description: Creates a new discussion post on a specific team's page within an organization.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The organization name is semantic content that only the user can specify; it is a natural language identifier for which organization to post in.
The title is semantic content that only the user can naturally compose for the discussion post.
The body content is semantic markdown that only the user can naturally author for the discussion post.

nodes.name: Create a discussion comment
nodes.description: Creates a new comment on an existing team discussion within a GitHub organization.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Organization name is a semantic identifier that only the user can naturally provide; it is not a reference produced by another tool.
The comment content is semantic text that the user must compose; it cannot be derived from tool outputs.

nodes.name: Create a fork
nodes.description: Creates a fork of an accessible repository, optionally into a specific organization, with a new name, or copying only the default branch.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is a reference to an existing repository that must be identified; while it's a string, the agent needs to know which repo to fork from
The owner is a reference to the GitHub user or organization that currently owns the repository; must be known to locate the source repo

nodes.name: Create a gist
nodes.description: Creates a new gist on GitHub with provided files, an optional description, and public/secret visibility.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The files parameter requires the user to provide the actual content and filenames for the gist. This is semantic content that cannot be automatically derived.

nodes.name: Create a gist comment
nodes.description: Creates a new comment on a specified GitHub gist.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The comment text is semantic content that only a human can naturally compose

nodes.name: Create a GitHub App from a manifest
nodes.description: Use this action to complete the GitHub App Manifest flow (step 3 of 3) by exchanging the temporary authorization `code` for the app's full configuration, including credentials and private key. The code is provided via redirect URL after creating the app through GitHub's web interface and must be used within 1 hour.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The authorization code is a temporary token provided by GitHub during the manifest flow and received via redirect URL. It is semantic content from the OAuth flow that the user must provide; it cannot be produced by another tool in this workflow.

nodes.name: Create a github pages deployment
nodes.description: Creates a GitHub Pages deployment for a repository using a specified artifact and OIDC token, provided GitHub Pages is enabled and the artifact (containing static assets) is accessible.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name must be obtained from prior tool output or user; commonly a producer_ref from list/get operations
Owner/organization name must be obtained from prior tool output or user; commonly a producer_ref from list/get operations

nodes.name: Create a github pages site

nodes.description: Tool to create a new user list on GitHub. Use when you need to organize and group GitHub users for easier tracking and management.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The name of the user list is semantic content that only a human can provide; it describes the list's purpose or category.

nodes.name: Create view for user project
nodes.description: Tool to create a new view in a user-owned GitHub project (Projects V2). Use when you need to add a customized view with specific layout, filters, and visible fields to organize project items. Note: This action works with GitHub Projects V2. The view can be configured with different layouts (table, board, roadmap) and optional filters. The visible_fields parameter is not applicable to roadmap layouts.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The view name is semantic content only a user can naturally provide
Layout type is a choice among predefined options (table, board, roadmap) that the user selects
The user_id/username is a reference identifier that identifies the project owner; typically obtained from a GitHub user lookup or list operation

nodes.name: Decline a repository invitation
nodes.description: Declines a specific, pending repository invitation for the authenticated user, identified by its `invitation_id`.

nodes.name: Delete access restrictions
nodes.description: Removes all user, team, and app-based access restrictions from a protected branch.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is a reference that typically comes from a prior tool that lists or retrieves repositories, or from user context. It identifies which repository's branch to modify.

Owner (username or organization) is a reference identifier that pairs with repo. While it could be user-provided directly, in realistic workflows it typically comes from a repository lookup or context.
Branch name is a semantic string that the user must specify to identify which branch's access restrictions to remove. No tool produces 'branch names' as a normalized entity.

nodes.name: Delete admin branch protection
nodes.description: Removes admin enforcement from a protected branch (branch name cannot contain wildcard characters) in a repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is a reference to a specific repository that must be identified; typically produced by a list/get repo tool or resolved via lookup
Repository owner (account name) is a reference that must be identified; typically produced by a list/get repo tool or resolved via lookup
Branch name is a semantic identifier provided by the user as plain text; no other tool produces branch names in a way that an agent would reference

nodes.name: Delete a file
nodes.description: Deletes a file by path from a GitHub repository. The file's blob SHA is automatically fetched if not provided.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository owner is a required identifier that typically comes from a prior lookup or user specification of a GitHub account
Repository name is a required identifier that typically comes from a prior lookup or user specification
File path is semantic content describing which file to delete; only the user knows the target location
Commit message is semantic content that describes the reason for deletion; only the user can provide meaningful context

nodes.name: Delete a package version for the authenticated user
nodes.description: Deletes an existing package version associated with the authenticated user.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Package type (maven, container, docker, etc.) is a semantic classification that only the user can specify; it is not an ID or reference produced by another tool.

nodes.name: Delete an app authorization
nodes.description: Deletes an OAuth application grant for a user, which revokes ALL OAuth tokens and their associated authorizations for the application. This endpoint is only accessible to OAuth App owners and requires Basic Authentication using the OAuth App's client_id and client_secret.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The OAuth App's client ID is a credential/identifier that only the app owner knows and must provide directly.

nodes.name: Delete an app token
nodes.description: Revokes a single OAuth access token for an OAuth App or GitHub App with OAuth authorization. IMPORTANT: This endpoint requires Basic Authentication using the OAuth App's client_id as username and client_secret as password. It CANNOT be called with personal access tokens, GitHub App installation tokens, or any other token type. You must be the OAuth App owner to use this endpoint.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The OAuth App's client ID is a credential/identifier that only the OAuth App owner knows and must provide; it's not produced by another tool in a typical agent workflow.

nodes.name: Delete a reference
nodes.description: Deletes a Git reference from a repository; 'ref' must be fully qualified (e.g., 'refs/heads/branch' or 'refs/tags/tag').

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is typically obtained from a prior tool that lists or identifies repositories, or from user selection of a specific repo context.
The repository owner (user or organization) is typically obtained from a prior tool or established in the workflow context.

nodes.name: Delete a release
nodes.description: Permanently deletes a specific release, its assets, and potentially its associated Git tag from a repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is typically obtained from a prior tool that lists or retrieves repositories, or from a search operation. While it can be user-provided as a natural language reference, in practice it's a structured identifier that downstream workflows depend on from repository discovery tools.
The account owner is a structured identifier typically resolved from repository metadata or user/organization lookups. It is paired with repo to form a unique repository reference.

nodes.name: Delete a release asset
nodes.description: Deletes a specific release asset from a GitHub repository; this action is idempotent.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is typically obtained from a prior tool that lists or creates repositories, or from user selection of a known repo.
Repository owner (username or organization) is typically obtained from context of a prior operation or user's own account.

nodes.name: Delete a release reaction
nodes.description: Deletes a reaction from a GitHub release, provided the repository, release, and reaction exist.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is a reference identifier that typically comes from a prior tool that listed or created repositories, or from a search/lookup tool.
The account owner is a reference identifier that pairs with the repository and typically comes from a prior tool or lookup.

nodes.name: Delete a repository
nodes.description: Permanently deletes the specified repository; this is a destructive, irreversible action that requires admin privileges for the repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is a reference to a specific repository that must either be provided by the user as a known identifier or produced by a prior tool that lists/creates repositories.
The repository owner (user or organization) is a reference that must either be provided by the user or resolved from a prior context where the owner was identified.

nodes.name: Delete a repository invitation
nodes.description: Deletes an active repository invitation, permanently revoking a user's access to collaborate on the specified repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is a reference to a repository that must exist; in agent workflows, the repo is typically identified by a prior tool that listed or created it.
The owner (username or organization) is a reference to an entity that must exist; typically resolved or provided by a prior tool or user lookup.

nodes.name: Delete a repository ruleset
nodes.description: Permanently deletes a repository ruleset.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is a canonical identifier that typically comes from a prior tool (list repos, get repo, create repo) or user lookup of repository by name.
Owner/username is a canonical identifier that typically comes from a prior tool or user lookup of account by name.

nodes.name: Delete a repository secret
nodes.description: Deletes a named GitHub Actions secret from a specified repository; this operation is destructive and idempotent, and requires the repository to exist.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is typically obtained from a prior operation that created or listed repositories, or must be provided by the user as a known reference.
The account owner is typically obtained from a prior operation or the authenticated user context, and is paired with repo to identify a repository.
The name of the secret to delete is semantic content that only the user can specify; it refers to a specific GitHub Actions secret they want to remove.

nodes.name: Delete a repository subscription
nodes.description: Deletes the authenticated user's subscription to a specified repository if it exists, effectively 'unwatching' it.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The owner is a GitHub account identifier that typically comes from a prior tool that lists or identifies repositories (e.g., GITHUB_LIST_REPOSITORIES). While it could be user-provided as a string, in agent workflows it is normally resolved or produced by a lookup/list tool.

The repository name is a reference that typically comes from a prior tool that lists or retrieves repositories. In agent workflows, repos are discovered via search or list operations rather than bare user input.

nodes.name: Delete a repository variable
nodes.description: Deletes a named variable (e.g., for GitHub Actions workflows) from a repository; the repository and the variable must already exist.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The variable name is semantic content that only a user can specify; it identifies which workflow variable to delete.
The repository name is a reference that must be known or produced by a prior tool that lists/creates/fetches repositories.
The repository owner (GitHub username or organization) is a reference that must be known or produced by a prior tool.

nodes.name: Delete a repository webhook
nodes.description: Deletes a specific webhook from a repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is typically obtained from a prior tool that lists or creates repositories, or identified via lookup from a search result.
Repository owner (GitHub account) is typically obtained from a prior tool result or user context, often paired with repo.

nodes.name: Delete a review comment for a pull request
nodes.description: Deletes a specific pull request review comment.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name must be obtained from a prior tool that lists or retrieves repositories, or from a user-provided search/lookup context.
The owner/organization name must be obtained from a prior tool that retrieves repository metadata or organization context.

nodes.name: Delete an artifact
nodes.description: Deletes a GitHub artifact by its ID within a repository, typically resulting in an empty response (HTTP 204 No Content) on success.

requiresUserInput.rationale: GitHub account owner; typically comes from a prior lookup or user selection of a repository/organization
Repository name; typically resolved via a prior repository lookup or selection

nodes.name: Delete a secret for the authenticated user
nodes.description: Deletes an existing Codespaces secret for the authenticated user by `secret_name`. This is a destructive and irreversible operation. Returns 404 if the secret does not exist.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The secret name is a semantic identifier chosen by the user. While it references an existing secret, the parameter itself is a user-provided string (the name of the secret to delete), not an ID produced by another tool.

nodes.name: Delete a self hosted runner from an organization
nodes.description: Forces the removal of a self-hosted runner from a GitHub organization. This endpoint can be used to completely remove the runner when the machine you were using no longer exists. The runner will be immediately disconnected from GitHub Actions. Requires admin:org scope and organization admin access. Returns 204 No Content on successful deletion.

nodes.name: Delete a self hosted runner from a repository
nodes.description: Removes a specific self-hosted runner (by `runner_id`) from a repository, if registered there; this is idempotent.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is a reference that typically comes from a prior tool that lists or retrieves repositories, or from user lookup of a repo by name/description.
The account owner is a reference typically obtained from a prior tool or user identification; it pairs with repo to uniquely identify a GitHub repository.

nodes.name: Delete a tag protection state for a repository
nodes.description: DEPRECATED: This API was sunset on August 30, 2024. Use GITHUB_DELETE_A_REPOSITORY_RULESET instead to manage tag protection rules via repository rulesets.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name must be obtained from a prior tool that lists or retrieves repositories, or be provided by the user as a known repository identifier.
The account owner must be obtained from a prior tool or provided by the user as a known owner identifier.

nodes.name: Delete a team
nodes.description: Deletes a team (and any child teams) from an organization.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The organization identifier must be known from a prior tool that lists or retrieves organization details, or be a GitHub organization the user is member of. Treated as a producer reference because it identifies a specific org entity.

nodes.name: Delete a thread subscription
nodes.description: Call this to mute a specific notification thread by deleting the user's subscription; notifications may still occur if the user is @mentioned, comments, or due to repository watch settings.

nodes.name: Delete an autolink reference from a repository
nodes.description: Deletes a specific autolink reference (which automatically links external resource IDs like JIRA-123 to URLs) from the specified repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is a required identifier that typically comes from a prior tool that listed or created repositories, or from user selection of a specific repo.
The owner (username or organization) is a required identifier that pairs with repo and typically comes from prior tool output or user context.

nodes.name: Delete a workflow run
nodes.description: Deletes a specific workflow run from a repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is typically obtained from a prior tool that lists or creates repositories, or from a search result.
Repository owner is typically obtained from a prior tool that lists repositories or from context of repository creation/lookup.

nodes.name: Delete branch protection
nodes.description: Removes all protection rules from a specific branch in a GitHub repository; the branch must currently have protection rules enabled.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is a reference to a GitHub repository that typically comes from a prior tool result (e.g., GITHUB_LIST_REPOS) or user lookup of repo by name.
The repository owner is a reference to a GitHub account/organization that typically comes from a prior tool result or user lookup.

The branch name is a semantic value that only the user can naturally specify (e.g., 'main', 'develop', 'feature-x'). While it could theoretically come from a listing tool, it's primarily user-provided semantic content.

nodes.name: Delete a code scanning analysis from a repository
nodes.description: Deletes a specific code scanning analysis by its ID from a repository; `confirm_delete` must be `true` if it's the last analysis of its type for a given tool and reference to prevent data loss.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is a reference to a specific repository resource; typically obtained from a repo listing or creation tool.
Account owner is a reference to a GitHub account; typically obtained from context or a user/org lookup tool.

nodes.name: Delete a codespace for the authenticated user

nodes.name: Delete a commit comment
nodes.description: Deletes a specific commit comment, identified by its `comment_id`, from the specified repository; the comment must exist.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is a reference that typically comes from a prior tool that listed or created repositories, or from user specification of an existing repo identity.
The owner/organization name is a reference that typically comes from a prior tool or user specification of an existing GitHub account identity.

nodes.name: Delete a commit comment reaction
nodes.description: Deletes a reaction from a commit comment in a GitHub repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is a reference that typically comes from a prior tool listing or creating a repository, or from user selection of a known repo.
Repository owner is a reference that typically comes from a prior tool or user context identifying the account owner.

nodes.name: Delete commit signature protection
nodes.description: Disables GPG commit signature protection for a specific branch in a GitHub repository, meaning commits pushed to this branch no longer require GPG signing.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is a GitHub repository identifier that typically comes from a prior tool that lists or creates repositories, or from user specification of an existing repo.
The owner is a GitHub account identifier (username or organization name) that is typically resolved via user specification or a prior lookup/list tool.
The branch name is a semantic string provided by the user specifying which branch to modify. While it could theoretically come from a prior tool listing branches, it is most naturally user-provided.

nodes.name: Delete a custom organization role
nodes.description: Deletes a custom organization role (which should not be actively assigned) by its ID; a 204 No Content response indicates success.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The organization name is a semantic identifier that only the user can specify; it is not produced by another tool.

nodes.name: Delete dependabot secret by name
nodes.description: Deletes a specific Dependabot secret, identified by its name, from a given repository if both the repository and secret exist.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is a reference that typically comes from a prior tool that lists or retrieves repositories, or could be a lookup_required if the user names it naturally. However, repo names are often directly provided by users as well. In agent workflows, this is typically a producer_ref when chained from repo discovery tools, but can also be user_provided. Classifying as producer_ref because it's an identifier that identifies a specific resource.

The owner (username or organization name) is a reference identifier. Like repo, it could come from prior lookups or be user-provided, but in a dependency graph it represents a specific account entity that must be resolved.

nodes.name: Delete a deploy key
nodes.description: Deletes a specific deploy key from a repository; to change a key's properties or access scope, the existing key must be deleted and a new one created.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name must be identified; typically obtained from a prior tool that listed or created repositories, or user provides it directly as a semantic reference.
Repository owner (account) must be identified; typically obtained from a prior tool or user context.

nodes.name: Delete a deployment
nodes.description: Permanently deletes a specified *inactive* deployment from a repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is a canonical identifier that typically comes from a prior tool that lists or creates repositories, or from a search/lookup of repos by name.
Owner (username) is a canonical identifier that typically comes from a prior tool or user context identifying the account owning the repository.

nodes.name: Delete a deployment branch policy
nodes.description: Deletes a specific deployment branch or tag policy, identified by its ID, from a given environment within a repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name must be obtained from a prior tool that lists or creates repositories, or from user knowledge of an existing repo.
Repository owner (account/organization name) must be known or obtained from a prior tool result.

nodes.name: Delete a discussion

requiresUserInput.rationale: Organization name is a semantic identifier provided by the user; no upstream tool produces it.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Array of email addresses is semantic content only the user can naturally provide; these are not IDs or references produced by other tools, but rather explicit email strings the user wants to delete.

nodes.name: Delete an environment
nodes.description: Deletes an existing deployment environment from an existing repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is typically obtained from a prior tool that lists or creates repositories, or user must specify it as a known repo identifier.
Owner (username or organization) is typically obtained from a prior tool or user context, identifying who owns the repository.

nodes.name: Delete an environment secret
nodes.description: Deletes an existing and accessible secret from a specified environment within a GitHub repository, returning an empty dictionary on success or error details otherwise.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is a reference typically produced by a list/get repository tool or lookup tool that resolves a repo by name/description.
Owner (username or organization) is a reference typically produced by a lookup or authentication context tool.

nodes.name: Delete an environment variable
nodes.description: Deletes a named environment variable from a specified, existing environment within a GitHub repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The environment variable name is semantic content that only the user can specify; it has no external reference or lookup requirement.
The repository name is a reference that must be obtained from a prior tool that lists or fetches repositories (e.g., GITHUB_LIST_REPOSITORIES or GITHUB_GET_REPOSITORY).
The owner/account name is a reference that must be obtained from a prior tool or context, typically from repository lookup or user profile.

nodes.name: Delete a gist

nodes.name: Delete GitHub Actions cache by ID

Owner (username or organization) is a reference typically resolved from context or a prior lookup of the repository.

nodes.name: Delete a GitHub Pages site
nodes.description: Deletes the GitHub Pages site for the specified repository; completes without error if no site is currently enabled.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name is a reference that typically comes from a prior tool that listed or created repositories, or from user specification of an existing repo identifier.
Owner (user or organization account) is a reference that typically comes from a prior tool or user specification of an existing account identifier.

nodes.name: Delete an issue comment

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name must be identified; typically comes from a prior tool that listed or created repos, or user provides as natural language requiring lookup.

nodes.name: Delete an issue comment reaction
nodes.description: Deletes a reaction from an issue comment in a repository; the repository, comment, and reaction must exist.

requiresUserInput.rationale: Repository name must be obtained from a prior tool that lists or retrieves repositories, or user provides it as a known repository identifier.

nodes.name: Delete an issue reaction
nodes.description: Permanently removes a specific reaction from an issue in a GitHub repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository owner is a GitHub account identifier that must be resolved or provided; typically a downstream output from repo discovery/listing tools.
The repository name is a reference identifier that pairs with owner; typically obtained from repo search/list tools.

nodes.name: Delete a label
nodes.description: Permanently removes an existing label from a repository.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The repository name is a required identifier that typically comes from a prior tool that listed or created repositories, or must be provided by the user as a concrete repo slug.

nodes.name: Delete a milestone

The repo name is a reference to a specific repository that must be known; typically paired with owner to identify a unique repository

requiresUserInput.rationale: The organization name is a semantic identifier provided by the user. While it could theoretically be produced by a tool that lists organizations, in typical workflows users know which organization they want to delete and provide it directly as a string.

nodes.name: Delete an organization secret

requiresUserInput.rationale: The organization name is a semantic identifier that only the user can naturally specify. While it could theoretically be produced by a tool listing organizations, the standard workflow is for the user to name the target organization.

nodes.name: Delete an organization variable

The variable name to delete is semantic content that only the user can specify; it is not an ID or reference produced by another tool.

nodes.name: Delete an organization webhook
nodes.description: Deletes a specific webhook, identified by `hook_id`, from an existing organization.

requiresUserInput.rationale: The organization name is a semantic identifier that the user must provide; it is not an ID produced by another tool.

nodes.name: Delete a codespace from the organization

nodes.name: Delete a package for an organization

nodes.name: Delete package version for an organization

nodes.name: Delete a pending review for a pull request
